person.name: Konrad Rzentarzewski
person.bio: I was there when the config burned. In the age before observability, chaos reigned. The servers howled, and nothing scaled but entropy. Then kube cattle were born — simple, nameless beasts born of children’s games. But they multiplied. And their hunger grew vast. Years passed. The sun revolved the Earth. Then the Earth revolved the Cloud. I grew tired of debugging in darkness. I longed for light. And logs. Preferably structured. That’s when I began preaching — not loudly, just… declaratively. One apply at a time. I walked among DevOps tribes, where bash begat shell, and shell begat pain. To them I brought ConfigMaps, and Secrets hidden in plain sight. Then came the mysterious YAML — a language too holy to lint — inscribed deep within the API’s own heart. Through toil and merge, the infrastructure became manifest. Pods floated on the waters. Deployments begat replicas. Helm bore Charts. Git begat Ops. What was once dev spun to prod. And every service had a name — and every name resolved. Unless external DNS was involved. And the Preachers said: \"Behold, our logs streameth clean.\" Thus began the age of the declarative. We once dwelled in darkness. Now, we scale planetary in full light.

Furthermore, he is one of the maintainers of Cloud-Barista, a multi-cloud management platform. Through my community activities, I have had the pleasure of meeting many talented and friendly contributors, and I look forward to continue developing these relationships. I believe that being a CNCF ambassador is an extraordinary opportunity for me to spread cloud-native technologies even further. person.name: Constanze Roedig
person.bio: Key Researcher & Lecturer, Public Speaker, Creator of the Software Bill of Behavior (SBoB) and maintainer of the Kubernetes Storm Center for cloudnative intelligence Backstory: Living intentionally is hard when routine takes control. I grew up wanting to be a scientist, studying Biochemistry, starting med school, and ultimately earning a PhD in General Relativity to explore how humans perceive 'reality.' When US funding cuts ended that path, I realized academia left me without agency.In the private sector, I discovered the value of risk. The Cloud Native paradigm let me experiment, fail fast, and try again without fear of lasting damage. In positive-sum collaborations, I saw how my algorithms could shape real-world outcomes, and how taking calculated risks created visible change for people and systems alike. This freedom to test ideas with relatively low cost revealed a new kind of agency, an immediacy and measurable connection to the meaning of work.My vision is to empower others to leverage cloud native technology with respect for the human element, security, and risk. Because having agency means creating reality for our society, for each one of us, one epsilon at a time.

She co-created, along with her team, the first Kubernetes Community Days in Colombia. This event paved the way for the expansion of Cloud Native Groups throughout the region. Cristher's foresight and operational expertise have been fundamental to the success of both Cuemby and the Cloud Native Latam Group. Recognizing the region's need for in-depth knowledge of cloud-native applications, Cristher championed the LFS250 (Linux Foundation Course) and administered the KCNA certifications donated by CNCF to the region. These two initiatives heightened awareness of cloud-native technologies and certifications, fortifying local proficiency in open-source solutions. She continues to promote and advocate for the recognition of the Cloud Native Computing Foundation (CNCF) in the region, addressing both business and community aspects. Cristher's leadership and operational prowess exemplify her unwavering commitment to fostering a thriving Latam tech community. https://community.cncf.io/cloud-native-latin-america/ https://community.cncf.io/cloud-native-medellin/ https://www.cncf.io/reports/kcd-colombia-2023/ https://www.cuemby.com/about-us https://www.linkedin.com/company/fundaci%C3%B3n-hispana-de-cloud-native/?viewAsMember=true https://community.cncf.io/cloud-native-colombia/ https://www.linkedin.com/showcase/kcd-colombia/?viewAsMember=truehttps://www.linkedin.com/showcase/kcd-colombiaWhy I am excited to be an ambassador: Becoming a CNCF ambassador empowers me to further my dedication to community growth in the LATAM region. Through this role, I am positioned to amplify awareness about these technologies and the transformative opportunities they introduce. person.name: Jonathan Bryce
person.bio: Jonathan Bryce is the Executive Director of Cloud & Infrastructure at the Linux Foundation, where he leads both the Cloud Native Computing Foundation (CNCF) and the OpenInfra Foundation—two of the largest and most influential open source communities in the world. With over 15 years of open source leadership, Jonathan co-founded the OpenStack project, guiding its evolution into a global ecosystem that supports a multi-billion-dollar market and serves as the de facto standard for open cloud infrastructure. Before his open source career, Jonathan spent over a decade in data center infrastructure and co-founded one of the industry's earliest cloud computing companies, which grew into a billion-dollar business following its acquisition by Rackspace. Passionate about open collaboration, infrastructure innovation, and empowering users, contributors, and ecosystems worldwide, Jonathan frequently speaks at international conferences on open source strategy, infrastructure modernization, cloud-native technologies, and open source AI initiatives.

person.name: Julius Da Silva Maia

With proven skills in resolving complex issues, implementing improvements, and collaborating with diverse stakeholders. Certified in Kubernetes, AWS, and Cisco, with a strong track record in analytical problem-solving and effective communication.

In this role, he oversees the strategy and development of Amazon Elastic Kubernetes Service (EKS) and Amazon Elastic Container Registry (ECR), helping organizations successfully run containerized applications at scale in the cloud.

I wanted to become a CNCF Ambassador to continue performing community activities involving the CNCF and open-source projects and host meetups and events like the KCD.

person.name: Harsha Kalyan
person.bio: Harsha Kalyanaraman is a Senior DevOps Engineer at Ubiety Technologies, where he designs and manages scalable cloud infrastructure across AWS and GCP environments. Six years ago, Harsha didn’t know the meaning of DevOps or software engineering—just a kid with dreams and an electronics background who came to the USA to pursue a Master’s degree in Computer Systems Engineering at Northeastern University. Guided by excellent mentors and faculty, he discovered his passion for infrastructure and cloud-native technologies, beginning his career at a small startup where he learned the fundamentals of DevOps practices. Today, he manages enterprise-level infrastructure, leveraging Docker, Kubernetes, and Terraform to orchestrate complex systems, and is now advancing his expertise through the Kubestronauts program to deepen his mastery of Kubernetes and contribute meaningfully to the cloud-native community. Harsha is eager to connect with fellow engineers, community members, and innovators to share experiences, learn from diverse perspectives, and grow together in the DevOps and Kubernetes ecosystem.

A published IEEE author and technical researcher, Pavan’s work dictates the strategic direction of distributed systems, specifically through his contributions to CNCF Dragonfly, KEDA, and OpenTelemetry. He is among an elite global cohort of engineers distinguished by a holistic mastery of cloud-native security, architecture, and high-performance computing (HPC) at the enterprise scale.

From on-premise HPCs with C# and MSSQL, to Hybrid cloud with Hadoop, Parquet, Hive, and Impala, to Cloud-hosted Spark and Microservices, and more recently to Cloud Native with Kubernetes and Databricks/Snowflake.
